eli3: Value at Risk (VaR) answers the question: 'What is the most I can lose in a given time period, with a given level of confidence?' For example, a 1-day 99% VaR of $1M means: 'On 99% of days, I will lose no more than $1M.' Equivalently, there's a 1% chance of losing more than $1M on any given day.

VaR is the standard risk measure used by banks and asset managers for regulatory capital (Basel III), internal risk limits, and portfolio risk reporting. It's intuitive, widely understood, and easy to aggregate across a portfolio. But it has serious limitations: it says nothing about what losses look like beyond the threshold (the 'tail'), and it can be gamed by strategies that have rare but catastrophic losses.

Three main methods to compute VaR: (1) Historical simulation: use actual historical returns; (2) Parametric (variance-covariance): assume normal distribution; (3) Monte Carlo: simulate thousands of scenarios. Each has trade-offs in accuracy, speed, and assumptions.

whenWhy.title: VaR computation methods compared
whenWhy.body: Historical simulation: rank the last T daily P&L observations from worst to best; 1-day 99% VaR = -1st percentile of the distribution = the 10th worst day (for T=1000). Pros: no distributional assumption; captures actual fat tails; easy to implement. Cons: limited by historical sample; can't model unprecedented scenarios; non-stationary (old crises may be stale). Parametric VaR: assume portfolio P&L ~ N(μ, σ²); VaR = -μ + z_α × σ where z_α = 2.33 for 99%. Pros: fast; requires only mean and covariance. Cons: normality understates tail risk; ignores fat tails and skewness. Monte Carlo: simulate N scenarios from a fitted distribution (or copula); compute VaR from the empirical distribution. Pros: handles non-linearity (options), captures fat tails if model is correct. Cons: computationally expensive; model-dependent.

whenWhy.title: Portfolio VaR and diversification benefit
whenWhy.body: For a portfolio of N assets: σ²_p = w'Σw. VaR_p = z_α × σ_p × portfolio_value. The diversification benefit: VaR_p < Σ_i VaR_i (portfolio VaR less than sum of individual VaRs). The diversification benefit = Σ_i VaR_i - VaR_p; measures how much risk is reduced by holding assets together. Example: two assets each with VaR = $1M; correlation ρ. Portfolio VaR = z_α × √2 × σ × √(1+ρ). At ρ = -1: portfolio VaR = 0 (perfect hedge). At ρ = 0: VaR = $1.41M (not $2M). At ρ = 1: VaR = $2M. Risk aggregation by business line: banks compute VaR for each desk; sum with diversification benefits to get firm-level VaR. Regulatory reporting: banks must report 1-day 99% VaR to regulators daily under Basel III market risk rules.

whenWhy.title: VaR backtesting: verifying the model
whenWhy.body: VaR backtesting: check whether the model's VaR predictions are consistent with actual losses. Under a correct 99% VaR model: the probability of an 'exception' (actual loss > VaR) on any day = 1%. Over T = 250 trading days: expected exceptions = 2.5. Basel III traffic light system: (1) Green zone: 0-4 exceptions in 250 days — model acceptable; (2) Yellow zone: 5-9 exceptions — investigate; (3) Red zone: 10+ exceptions — model rejected, capital multiplier increased (penalty of 3-4×). Statistical test (Kupiec test): H0: exception rate = 1%; test statistic LR = -2 ln[(1-p)^{T-N} × p^N] + 2 ln[(1-N/T)^{T-N} × (N/T)^N], where N = actual exceptions, T = total days. If VaR consistently under-reports risk, capital requirements are too low — the 2008 crisis revealed that bank VaR models dramatically underestimated tail risk.

deepDiveIntro: VaR at confidence level alpha is the loss threshold such that the portfolio suffers a greater loss than this with probability one minus alpha. For a continuous profit-and-loss distribution it equals the negative of the alpha quantile of that distribution. Scaling from one day to multiple days under independent returns uses the square-root-of-time rule, but this understates risk when returns have fat tails or autocorrelation. Basel III requires banks to report ten-day 99% VaR for market risk capital and adds a stressed VaR component computed over the worst twelve-month historical period.

deepDive.heading: Limitations of VaR and the Basel Shift to ES
deepDive.body: VaR has four critical weaknesses. First, it is not sub-additive: the VaR of a combined portfolio can exceed the sum of individual VaRs for fat-tailed distributions, meaning it can mathematically penalize diversification. Second, it ignores tail shape: two portfolios with identical VaR can have very different losses beyond the threshold. Third, it is gameable: selling deep out-of-the-money options produces rare but catastrophic losses that VaR misses entirely. Fourth, it is not a coherent risk measure (Artzner et al. 1999). Basel III's Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (2016) replaced VaR with Expected Shortfall at 97.5% confidence, which averages losses beyond the threshold and addresses all four limitations. [1]

deepDive.heading: Filtered Historical Simulation
deepDive.body: Standard historical simulation treats every past day as equally likely, which understates risk when current volatility is elevated and overstates it when markets are calm. Filtered Historical Simulation fixes this by estimating a GARCH model per risk factor, dividing historical returns by their estimated conditional standard deviations to produce approximately independent standardized residuals, resampling those residuals, and rescaling by the current GARCH volatility estimate. The resulting scenarios inherit fat tails from history while adapting to today's volatility regime — producing higher VaR in stressed markets and lower VaR in calm ones. [2]

deepDive.heading: VaR in Asset Management: Tracking Error vs. Absolute VaR
deepDive.body: Asset managers use two VaR concepts. Absolute VaR measures maximum loss in dollar terms and is relevant for hedge funds and multi-asset funds with fixed drawdown limits. Relative VaR, usually called tracking error, measures the volatility of portfolio returns minus benchmark returns and is the standard risk measure for long-only equity funds. European UCITS regulation requires absolute VaR funds to keep twenty-day 99% VaR below 20% of net asset value, while relative VaR funds must keep the ratio of portfolio VaR to benchmark VaR below two. Daily risk reports decompose active risk into contributions from market beta, sector tilts, factor exposures, and idiosyncratic positions. [1]

takeaways: 1-day 99% VaR: maximum expected daily loss on 99% of days; 1% chance of exceeding it. [1]
Historical simulation: no distributional assumption; parametric: fast but assumes normality; MC: flexible but model-dependent. [1]
VaR backtesting: 10+ exceptions in 250 days = red zone; model rejected, capital penalty applied. [1]
VaR limitations: not sub-additive, ignores tail shape, gameable — Basel FRTB replaced VaR with ES (CVaR) at 97.5%. [1]
FHS: GARCH-filter returns to i.i.d., bootstrap residuals, scale by current vol — adapts to current regime. [2]

edgeCases.q: Can VaR increase when you add a hedge to your portfolio?
edgeCases.a: Counterintuitively, yes — VaR is not sub-additive for non-elliptical distributions. Example: a portfolio with a very stable positive return but rare large loss can have low VaR; adding a hedge (which has a small negative correlation with the main risk) could shift the loss distribution so the 1st percentile changes in a surprising direction. More practically: VaR on correlated assets is generally sub-additive; but for portfolios with non-linear payoffs (options) or fat-tailed distributions, adding a new position can increase VaR even if it reduces overall portfolio risk. This sub-additivity failure is why regulators shifted to Expected Shortfall, which is always sub-additive and thus always rewards diversification/hedging.

edgeCases.q: How do you compute VaR for a portfolio containing options?
edgeCases.a: Options are non-linear — delta approximation understates their risk. Full valuation Monte Carlo: (1) Simulate N scenarios for all risk factors (equity price, vol, rates, FX); (2) For each scenario, fully reprice each option using the Black-Scholes (or Heston, etc.) formula; (3) Compute full portfolio P&L for each scenario; (4) VaR = 1st percentile of P&L distribution. Delta-gamma approximation (faster): ΔP ≈ Δ × ΔS + ½ Γ × ΔS² + Θ × Δt + Vega × Δσ; compute VaR analytically from this quadratic approximation. Accuracy: delta-gamma is good for small moves (day-horizon VaR); fails for large moves where higher-order terms matter. Jump risk: for portfolios with barrier or path-dependent options, even delta-gamma-vega fails; Monte Carlo with full revaluation is required.

sources.label: Jorion, P. — Value at Risk: The New Benchmark for Managing Financial Risk (3rd ed.), McGraw-Hill
Barone-Adesi, G., Giannopoulos, K. & Vosper, L. (1999) — VaR Without Correlations for Portfolios of Derivative Securities, JFQA
